skill.name: firebase-security-rules-auditor
skill.description: Audit Firebase Firestore security rules to identify vulnerabilities, privilege escalation risks, and logic flaws before deployment.
skill.summary: A defensive security auditor that evaluates Firestore security rules against a rigorous penetration-testing checklist.
skill.author: firebase

security_audit.summary: All static findings were false positives. The external_commands alerts matched Markdown backticks in documentation text, not shell execution. The weak-cryptography alerts triggered on the word 'secure' in a description. The system-reconnaissance alerts matched security terminology such as 'bypass' inside a defensive audit checklist. The skill contains no executable code, network requests, file system operations, or environment access. It is a pure prompt template for defensive security auditing of Firestore rules.

content.value_statement: Misconfigured Firestore security rules can expose sensitive data or allow privilege escalation. This skill acts as a senior penetration tester to find logic flaws, update bypasses, and missing ownership checks before attackers do.
actual_capabilities: Detect update bypass vulnerabilities where create and update rules diverge
Identify reliance on user-provided data for authority fields such as role or ownerId
Flag missing type checks and size limits that enable resource exhaustion
Spot missing ownership checks when field-level restrictions are used without identity verification
Score rule sets on a 1-5 scale with severity-rated findings and remediation advice
Return structured JSON assessments suitable for CI/CD gates
limitations: Analyzes rule text only; cannot test runtime behavior against a live database
Does not generate fixed rule code; provides recommendations only
Assumes standard Firestore rule syntax; custom functions may need manual review

use_cases.title: Pre-deployment rule review
use_cases.description: Developers paste proposed Firestore rules before merging to catch privilege escalation or data leaks.

use_cases.title: Security audit for existing apps
use_cases.description: Security engineers audit production rule sets to verify they still match evolving business logic and threat models.

use_cases.title: CI/CD compliance gate
use_cases.description: DevOps teams integrate the skill into pipelines to block deployments with critical or major rule vulnerabilities.

prompt_templates.title: Quick health check
prompt_templates.prompt:
Audit these Firestore security rules and give me a score from 1 to 5. List any critical or major issues.

```
[Paste rules here]
```

prompt_templates.title: Full red-team assessment
prompt_templates.prompt:
Act as a senior penetration tester. Analyze the following Firestore rules for update bypasses, authority source flaws, business logic gaps, storage abuse, type safety, and ownership checks. Return a JSON report with score, summary, and findings.

```
[Paste rules here]
```

prompt_templates.title: Compare old vs new rules
prompt_templates.prompt:
I updated my Firestore rules. Compare the old and new versions and tell me if the changes introduced new vulnerabilities or fixed existing ones.

Old rules:
```
[Paste old rules]
```

New rules:
```
[Paste new rules]
```

prompt_templates.title: Custom business logic validation
prompt_templates.prompt:
Our app is a [describe app]. These are our Firestore rules. Verify that the rules actually support our business logic and do not force insecure workarounds.

App description: [details]

Rules:
```
[Paste rules here]
```

output_examples.input: Audit a rule set that allows any authenticated user to update a document using hasOnly(['name'])
output_examples.output:
Score: 2 (Major)

Finding: Missing ownership check combined with hasOnly(). Any authenticated user can update the name field on another user's document because there is no resource.data.uid == request.auth.uid check.

Recommendation: Add an ownership or role verification before allowing updates.

output_examples.input: Audit rules for a chat app where create requires auth but update has no size limits
output_examples.output:
Score: 3 (Moderate)

Finding: Resource Exhaustion/DoS risk. Update rules do not enforce string length or array size limits on message fields.

Finding: Update Bypass. A user could create a valid message and then update it to exceed expected size boundaries.

Recommendation: Add validation rules for maximum string length and array size on both create and update.

best_practices: Run this audit every time Firestore rules change, even for small patches
Pair the audit with integration tests that exercise create, update, and delete paths
Document business logic assumptions so the auditor can verify rule-to-app alignment
anti_patterns: Relying solely on client-side validation because the rules look complex
Using request.resource.data.role without server-side verification or secure ACLs
Copying example rules from tutorials without adapting ownership checks to your data model

faq.question: Does this skill modify my Firebase project?
faq.answer: No. The skill only reads and analyzes the rule text you provide. It does not connect to Firebase or change any project settings.

faq.question: Can it guarantee my rules are secure?
faq.answer: No tool can guarantee security. The skill applies a thorough checklist, but you should still perform integration testing and periodic expert reviews.

faq.question: What Firestore features does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers create, read, update, and delete rules, as well as functions, auth checks, field validation, and ownership logic.

faq.question: Does it support Firebase Realtime Database rules?
faq.answer: No. This skill is designed specifically for Firestore security rules. Realtime Database uses a different syntax and model.

faq.question: How should I use the JSON output?
faq.answer: Parse the JSON to extract the score and findings list. You can use the score as a CI/CD gate or feed findings into a ticketing system.

faq.question: Why did it flag a rule that looks safe?
faq.answer: The skill uses an adversarial mindset. A rule may look correct but still allow an update bypass or rely on user-controlled data. Review the specific finding for details.
